issue_title: Build issues on Windows using VS2015
issue_body:
Hi all,

I've been having a problem building `pyvex` on Windows 10 with Visual Studio 2015 (no Cygwin or MinGW). The issue is related to how `make_ffi.py` is invoking preprocessing tools (for more context on how I disovered it, see angr/angr-dev#16).

If I run `python setup.py develop` in the `pyvex` source tree from a VS2015 developer prompt, I get the following exception and stack trace:

```
Creating CFFI defs file
No handlers could be found for logger "cffier"
Exception('Couldn\'t process pyvex headers.Please set CPP environmental variable to local path of "cpp".Note that "cpp" and "g++" are different.',)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "setup.py", line 143, in <module>
    'pyvex': ['lib/*', 'include/*']
  File "c:\python27\Lib\distutils\core.py", line 151, in setup
    dist.run_commands()
  File "c:\python27\Lib\distutils\dist.py", line 953, in run_commands
    self.run_command(cmd)
  File "c:\python27\Lib\distutils\dist.py", line 972, in run_command
    cmd_obj.run()
  File "setup.py", line 112, in run
    self.execute(_build_ffi, (), msg="Creating CFFI defs file")
  File "c:\python27\Lib\distutils\cmd.py", line 349, in execute
    util.execute(func, args, msg, dry_run=self.dry_run)
  File "c:\python27\Lib\distutils\util.py", line 309, in execute
    func(*args)
  File "setup.py", line 90, in _build_ffi
    make_ffi.doit(os.path.join(VEX_PATH,'pub'))
  File "c:\src\pyvex\make_ffi.py", line 88, in doit
    "Note that \"cpp\" and \"g++\" are different."
Exception: Couldn't process pyvex headers.Please set CPP environmental variable to local path of "cpp".Note that "cpp" and "g++" are different.
```
Currently, `make_ffi.py` tries a whole bunch of preprocessors in a loop (starting at line [66](https://github.com/angr/pyvex/blob/fb0b164/make_ffi.py#L66)), the first of which is VS's `cl` with the relevant arguments appended. This should (and in fact does) work fine in terms of generating the correct output... however, the current heuristic for detecting failure (at line [75](https://github.com/angr/pyvex/blob/fb0b164/make_ffi.py#L75)) assumes that `stderr` will be `None` if no problems were encountered. This assumption doesn't hold for `cl`, as it prints a version string to `stderr` (whereas the rest of the output goes to `stdout`). 

An obvious fix for `cl` (and hence for VS2015 support) would be to remove the `or stderr` clause from the if statement at line 75, but I don't know if this would then break appropriate error detection for other tools like `cpp`, `clang` et al. If I get time later, I'll try and test the results with some of those other tools, but not sure when I'll get around to that. 

Any advice/comments/suggestions appreciated! If someone has authoritative guidance on what patch would be most appropriate for all the possible preprocessing tools, I'm happy to submit the relevant PR. 

cheers,
Ben

```

This I believe is coming from the fact that in [Proxy.py:__recursive_strip#L531](https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/develop/python/Ganga/GPIDev/Base/Proxy.py#L531) we explicitly check that the value is not a string i.e. type `str`. This doesn't catch the case where the value is a unicode string i.e. `u'HelloWorld'` which is of type `unicode`. To make sure that we catch both I recommend changing this to check for type `basestring` which will catch both as below:

```python
>>> issubclass(unicode, str)
False
>>> issubclass(unicode, basestring)
True
>>> issubclass(str, basestring)
True
```
---
**caveat** I Don't know that this is causing the problem yet, it's just my best guess. I'll check if this small patch fixes things and submit a PR if so.

issue_title: Feature Request: Discover by default ports
issue_body:
I don't know if it has been discussed before, but I had the idea of discovering services by the ports they use by default. Is this a thing netdisco should do? The basic concept of what I mean:

```python
#!/usr/bin/python3
import asyncio
import time

NOW = time.time()
DESTS = ["192.168.1.{}".format(i) for i in range(1, 255)]
PORTS = {
    1883: "mqtt",
    2000: "homematic-wired",
    2001: "homematic-wireless",
    2010: "homematic-ip",
    3306: "mysql",
    5432: "postgres",
    6466: "androidtv",
    8086: "influxdb",
    8883: "mqtts",
    # etc.
}

async def check_port(ip, port, loop):
    try:
        reader, writer = await asyncio.open_connection(ip, port, loop=loop)
        print(ip, port, PORTS[port])
        writer.close()
        return (ip, port, True)
    except:
        return (ip, port, False)

async def run(dests, ports, loop):
    tasks = [asyncio.ensure_future(check_port(d, p, loop)) for d in dests for p in ports]
    responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    return responses

LOOP = asyncio.get_event_loop()
FUTURE = asyncio.ensure_future(run(DESTS, PORTS.keys(), LOOP))
LOOP.run_until_complete(FUTURE)
print('#'*50)
print('Total time: ', time.time() - NOW)
```
(modified version of [this](http://gunhanoral.com/python/2017/07/04/async-port-check.html))

It takes me ~130 seconds to scan my class C network, but the main relevant services actually show up rather quickly. I wouldn't go as far as expecting the results to be 100% accurate. But maybe services discovered this way could show a proposed HASS component (with link to documentation) that might work together with the discovered service.

That way especially newbies could be informed, that they have something that might be integrated into HASS, which they have not thought before because they haven't looked through all the components HASS supports. I would consider this a workaround for services which don't publish themselves via mDNS etc.

issue_title: Number support
issue_body:
For an intent parser I created, where you could ask the timetable for a specific bus line, I had to write down the possible numbers for bus lines. But then, typing real numbers from the command line, e.g. `when is the next bus sixty-one leaving from home?` starts to be annoying.

Would it be possible to build in a support for numbers, somehow? I know of the library [inflect](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/inflect) that translates integers to text, which would help quite a bit on this. There is also [pynum2word](https://sourceforge.net/projects/pynum2word/).

I think that being able to specify a new sort of entity (i.e. a numerical entity) would be nice. Something where you specify a range, say 1 to 100, and it will then understand both `1` and `sixty-one`, preferably returning the integer version in the json structure.

issue_body:
I would like to utilize the MlpPolicy with AC2:

    class MlpPolicyCustom(object):
        def __init__(self, sess, ob_space, ac_space, nbatch, nsteps, reuse=False): #pylint: disable=W0613
            ob_shape = (nbatch,) + ob_space.shape
            actdim = ac_space.n
            X = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, ob_shape, name='Ob') #obs
            with tf.variable_scope("model", reuse=reuse):
                activ = tf.tanh
                h1 = activ(fc(X, 'pi_fc1', nh=64, init_scale=np.sqrt(2)))
                h2 = activ(fc(h1, 'pi_fc2', nh=64, init_scale=np.sqrt(2)))
                pi = fc(h2, 'pi', actdim, init_scale=0.01)
                h1 = activ(fc(X, 'vf_fc1', nh=64, init_scale=np.sqrt(2)))
                h2 = activ(fc(h1, 'vf_fc2', nh=64, init_scale=np.sqrt(2)))
                vf = fc(h2, 'vf', 1)[:,0]
                logstd = tf.get_variable(name="logstd", shape=[1, actdim],
                    initializer=tf.zeros_initializer())

            pdparam = tf.concat([pi, pi * 0.0 + logstd], axis=1)

            self.pdtype = make_pdtype(ac_space)
            self.pd = self.pdtype.pdfromflat(pdparam)

            a0 = self.pd.sample()
            neglogp0 = self.pd.neglogp(a0)
            self.initial_state = None

            def step(ob, *_args, **_kwargs):
                a, v, neglogp = sess.run([a0, vf, neglogp0], {X:ob})
                print("a:", a)
                return a, v, self.initial_state, neglogp

            def value(ob, *_args, **_kwargs):
                return sess.run(vf, {X:ob})

            self.X = X
            self.pi = pi
            self.vf = vf
            self.step = step
            self.value = value

However the following line concatenates the policy tensor along `axis=1`, changing my `ac_space` shape from `shape=(2,10)` to `shape=(2,20)`:

issue_title: URL_DETECTOR failed to detect complex data uris
issue_body:
Now `URL_DETECTOR = r"""url\((['"]){0,1}\s*(.*?)["']{0,1}\)"""`

If the url is something like `url("data:image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8,%3Csvg%20viewBox%3D'0%200%20120%20120'%20xmlns%3D'http%3A%2F%2Fwww.w3.org%2F2000%2Fsvg'%20xmlns%3Axlink%3D'http%3A%2F%2Fwww.w3.org%2F1999%2Fxlink'%3E%3Cdefs%3E%3Cline%20id%3D'l'%20x1%3D'60'%20x2%3D'60'%20y1%3D'7'%20y2%3D'27'%20stroke%3D'%236c6c6c'%20stroke-width%3D'11'%20stroke-linecap%3D'round'%2F%3E%3C%2Fdefs%3E%3Cg%3E%3Cuse%20xlink%3Ahref%3D'%23l'%20opacity%3D'.27'%2F%3E%3Cuse%20xlink%3Ahref%3D'%23l'%20opacity%3D'.27'%20transform%3D'rotate(30%2060%2C60)'%2F%3E%3C%2Fg%3E%3C%2Fsvg%3E");`

It will only match `data:image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8,%3Csvg%20viewBox%3D'0%200%20120%20120'%20xmlns%3D'http%3A%2F%2Fwww.w3.org%2F2000%2Fsvg'%20xmlns%3Axlink%3D'http%3A%2F%2Fwww.w3.org%2F1999%2Fxlink'%3E%3Cdefs%3E%3Cline%20id%3D'l'%20x1%3D'60'%20x2%3D'60'%20y1%3D'7'%20y2%3D'27'%20stroke%3D'%236c6c6c'%20stroke-width%3D'11'%20stroke-linecap%3D'round'%2F%3E%3C%2Fdefs%3E%3Cg%3E%3Cuse%20xlink%3Ahref%3D'%23l'%20opacity%3D'.27'%2F%3E%3Cuse%20xlink%3Ahref%3D'%23l'%20opacity%3D'.27'%20transform%3D'rotate(30%2060%2C60` because of the presence of `)'`

`URL_DETECTOR` cannot distinguish whether the uri is quoted by single quotation marks or double quotation marks

There is an example of how to match a quoted string in [Python documentation ](https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html):
>Named groups can be referenced in three contexts. If the pattern is (?P<quote>['"]).*?(?P=quote) (i.e. matching a string quoted with either single or double quotes):

So `URL_DETECTOR` will become `r"""url\((?P<quote>['"]{0,1})\s*(?P<uri>.*?)(?P=quote)\)"""`
and we can access the uri part by `matched.group('uri')`

issue_title: Tensorboard ignores misspelled flags
issue_body:
Tensorboard silently ignores misspelled flags. Whatever flag you pass to tensorboard it swallows it without even blinking. This might seem like a small problem, but it causes some real-life problems.

A real-life example - this caused significant cost increase in our case for our tensorboard installation in GCP. We run it in kubernetes cluster and we used the https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-tensorboard project as a base for our installation. Unfortunately some time recently the name of --reload-frequency parameter has changed to --reload-interval (which is BTW. much better name for what it was) but the appengine-tensorboard project have still the old parameter in configuration (I created pull request https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-tensorboard/pull/1 to fix it).

Due to silent swallowing of misspelled parameters, we have not even noticed that the parameter name has changed since the appengine-tensorboard project was released (actually there was even weird inconsistency in the appengine-tensorboard project that in one place RELOAD_INTERVAL variable was mentioned but not used). 

All-in-all - it would have been much better if tensorboard - following good practice of about few hundred thousands of other projects ;) - would fail if you specify wrong parameter.

issue_title: analysis: Thread1.1 6.3.2 Network data update
issue_body: 6.3.2 test case fails when checking if CIDs corresponding to all configured on-mesh prefix are present in Address Registration TLV in Child Update Request message. Currently for sleepy end device, after it obtains the new network data(i.e. on-mesh prefix propagated from border router), it will send two child update request messages continuously to its parent. The first child update message occurs in `HandleNetworkDataUpdate` after sleepy end device set the new network data; and the second one occurs in `HandleNetifStateChanged` after a new IPv6 unicast address added on thread interface. Harness will locate the first child update message after leader multicast a Data Response with new configured on-mesh prefix(i.e. 2001::/64). However, for the first child update message, Address Registration TLV still does not include the new configured IPv6 unicast address, hence, it does not include CID neither. The screenshot as below: Notice that Thread 1.1 spec 4.7.3 states: "A child update Request may be sent by a child in the following circumstances: To modify its mode, timeout duration, and/or address registration" @jwhui @librasungirl Is it essential to send the first child update request in `HandleNetworkDataUpdate` for a sleepy end device? or if there is another scenario that require to send the child update request in `HandleNetworkDataUpdate`, please let me know. Thanks.

issue_title: MT-Safe platform abstraction
issue_body:
OpenThread takes advantage of a single threaded view of the processor. Either the User application is running or the stack is running. neither can preempt the other. On a multi-threaded architecture such as an RTOS, the preemption can happen at any time. This becomes an issue if a stack tasklet in running in the stack processing RTOS Task, and a context switch occurs to another RTOS task that tries to call one of the OpenThread APIs.

The pending Riot port of OpenThread (https://github.com/RIOT-OS/RIOT/pull/5552) uses a mutex to lock and unlock the stack functionality. The onerous of locking and unlocking the mutex falls on the caller, and it is assumed that all calls to the OpenThread APIs are protected by this mutex.

It would be good to have a platform abstraction interface for MT environments to make the `C` APIs MT-Safe.

I think these two functions would be enough:
```c
/**
 * Enters the mutex gate for execution of OpenThread APIs.
 *
 * @return A key to be passed to the matching leave function.
 */
uintptr_t otApiEnter(void);

/**
 * Leaves the mutex gate for execution of OpenThread APIs.
 *
 * @param [in] key The value returned by the corresponding lock call.
 */
void otApiLeave(uintptr_t key);
```
And adding them to the beginning and end of the `C` API functions in `src/core/api` should be sufficient to make the `C` APIs MT-Safe.

The mutex object that backs these APIs would have to be a recursive mutex. Meaning that the same task can lock the mutex multiple times, provided they are unlocked in the same order. This would allow callback functions to use the `C` APIs, even though they are being called from a Task that has already locked the mutex.

issue_title: Windows Standalone Support
issue_body:
So @Swanson-Hysell pointed out to me that the Windows Standalone is lagging far behind the rest of the project on releases. I've understood that it's been hard for @moonshoes87 to maintain it because as everyone knows py2exe can be a pain. I thought I'd look into compiling on my computer a standalone version of Pmag GUI using Pyinstaller which doesn't allow cross compiling, but as I have a windows distribution on half my hard drive this isn't an issue. Now 2 things came out of this:

* First occasionally Pmag GUI needs to know where some data files are so I couldn't just make a single binary I had to bundle those text files along with it and rewrite a little code to make sure it could always find it. Thanks to the good developing practices used for this import though it was relatively easy.
* Second Pyinstaller is not fast. It doesn't compile the python down to machine code in any way. As far as I can tell it just bundles a binary python interpreter in with binaries of the python files used and then on execution kind of on the run unpacks them and parses them through the bundled interpreter. It takes near a full 40 seconds to launch on my laptop.

So I guess the point of this issue is I would like to help continue support of a windows and maybe a linux binary as it seems a good way to help newcomers to the project and people who just want the program to run in their lab. Though this is a less than ideal binary as it has to be run next to the data models file 
I've included in the attached google drive link to an archive. Can those with the ability to run windows binaries check this out, and if it seems to be working @moonshoes87 could we work on making this binary a new release for the Windows standalone so that it's more up to date with the current state of the project? Also if anyone has any suggestions for optimizing this binary I'd be willing to try any ideas.

https://drive.google.com/file/d/0ByqHHbKOuiclOXc2QzVkYXJ2a2s/view?usp=sharing

issue_title: Cannot find features or steps if the Django project is not the project root
issue_body:
If I have a project where the root directory is not the same as the Django project root directory, then aloe fails to find feature and step files in the Django project's apps.

For example, with the following project layout, aloe won't find `awesome.feature` because there is no `__init__.py` in the `django_stuff` directory:

```bash
$ tree project_root
project_root
└── django_stuff
    ├── db.sqlite3
    ├── manage.py
    ├── django_app
    │   ├── admin.py
    │   ├── apps.py
    │   ├── features
    │   │   └── awesome.feature
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── models.py
    │   ├── tests.py
    │   └── views.py
    └── django_stuff_project
        ├── __init__.py
        ├── settings.py
        ├── urls.py
        └── wsgi.py
```

I can resolve this by adding a `__init__.py` file to `django_stuff`, the Django project root, however this causes issues with Django itself: Django will consider the Django project root itself to be an "app", and will complain that models have been declared multiple times in different apps. See http://stackoverflow.com/questions/26591399/django-1-7-conflicting-models and https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/22280 for examples of that problem.

issue_title: Definition of character n-gram in fasttext
issue_body:
Hi. I have read the papers and I can't see something where a definition of "character n-gram" is provided. A possible example definition could be: given a word, e.g. "genomics", the corresponding set of 3-grams is given by `G_0={gen, eno, nom, omi, mic, ics}`. Another possible definition could be, for the same example word: `G_1={gen,omi,cs\0}`. Please, can someone clear what exactly "n-gram" is referring to? Given that the score function (the argument of the output layer softmax) takes the sum of the n-gram hidden embeddings, a possible and intuitive choice is `G_1`, but I'm not sure. 

Once defined what an n-gram actually is, the set `G` contains different `N=1,2,..,n,...-grams` at the same time? or a set `G` is generated independently for each `n`? 

Thanks :)

from csvkit.utilities.csvcut import CSVCut as c
c('-c 2 x4.csv'.split(' ')).main()
```

This produced the expected output in what looks like stdout.

When I try to capture the output, this simple approach fails:

```
result = c('-c 2 x4.csv'.split(' ')).main()
```
... the output is still printed and `x` is empty.

I tried creating a context manager that would capture stdout, with no luck.

How do you recommend the utilities be called from within another Python module?

Thanks

issue_title: SSL certificate hostname mismatch is checked only once
issue_body: For more info, see https://github.com/jcgregorio/httplib2/issues/243.

issue_title: Add DictionaryLearning
issue_body: Would be nice to have [scikit-learn's DictionaryLearning]( http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.decomposition.DictionaryLearning.html ) wrapped up for use with large Dask Arrays.

issue_title: @wraps breaks introspection
issue_body:
https://github.com/dask/dask-ml/blob/cb3d0ea392a277cade35405f6b156f22a1616551/dask_ml/datasets.py#L11

The use of `@wraps` here leads to wrong behavior of some introspection tools, like `??` in Jupyter (will show you the code for the sklearn version).

I feel that it's best to wrap thing manually (assigning the `__name__`, `__doc__` and `__module__` attributes for `inner` manually), after line 17, before the `return`.

This issue is making some unpleasant workarounds necessary in the `xarray_filters` codebase (same `datasets.py` file) (where we are trying to give more helpful docstrings users beyond what's in sklearn). The issue for me revolves around knowing whether I'm going to wrap the `make_blobs` from `dask_ml` or from `sklearn`. I could do it by inspecting the signature, or the `__module__` attribute, but those attributes are set equal to the ones in sklearn right now.

I'll put links to relevants parts of the `xarray_filters` codebase here as soon as I push the new code up.

issue_title: The import() method should be a class method
issue_body: The import() method is a kind of constructor. Please make it a class method.

issue_title: adding -delete option to remove the cert files
issue_labels: [{'id': 210831837, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/cert%20management', 'name': 'cert management', 'color': '00dece', 'default': False}, {'id': 281347824, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/has%20pr', 'name': 'has pr', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}, {'id': 483030079, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/soccer%20ball', 'name': 'soccer ball', 'color': '303030', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Hello,

Do you think you can add an -delete option to let's encrypt?

My context is this: 
From a hosting control panel, users will be able to create an owncloud instance.
When deleting their owncloud instance, I should delete also the certificates files in /etc/letsencrypt folder (I'm not talking about revocation as I can let the certs expire).

Deleting them seems important as by not deleting myself the files in /etc/letsencrypt/{archives,live,renewal}, if I understand correctly, the renewal of the certs will renew all the certs, included the certs for (sub)domains that don't exist anymore.

I must say I'm not sure how the renewal system works, if it tests the domain validity or the host file presence before renewing it. But still, you can have a host file for a domain name that doesn't exist anymore (not very good, but it can happen) and you can have a catchall for subdomains that show a generic page or use the (sub)domain for another purpose.

The only post I found on that topic (https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/correct-way-to-completely-remove-issued-certificate-s-for-a-domain/7409/4) doesn't have an answer on this problem.

If I manage the deletion by myself, deleting all the files /etc/letsencrypt/ subdirs, what about if something changes in the way let's encrypt manage the files (names / placements / ..).
The script will be broken.

Maybe there is another way:
If, by using the revoke option, the renewal feature won't renew the revoked certs, I can live with that.
But in that case, if later, a user want to re-use the same (sub)domain, is it possible to reactivate the certs? (didn't see anything about that in the doc. The renew feature doesn't say anything about this case)

So, is it possible to add such a feature?

And by the way, let's encrypt is a wonderful project. Thanks to you all.

issue_title: Unreliable polling timeout
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Users are reporting problems with our polling timeout that defaults to 10 seconds (#1064 #1058 and also on the gitter channel). This behavior is even more surprising because the polling fail, but the transaction did not, so the transaction will complete after a few more seconds and the second request from the use will fail again.

## Solution

My proposed solution is to remove *all timeouts* from the "middleware", pushing the timeouts to the edges, and only err when the transaction actually failed (either it was mined and the status is failure or it was removed from the transactions poll). This change would affect primarily the blockchain proxies that currently expose a synchronous interface.

issue_title: Design issue: Post-generation declaration and overrides
issue_body:
As part of an ongoing refactor of factory_boy's core engine (which should remove lots of quirks and asymmetry between different kinds of declarations), we need to decide how to handle a specific case with post-generation declaration.

## Context

Suppose we have the following factory:

```python
class UserFactory(factorry.Factory):
    class Meta:
        model = User

    username = factory.Faker('user_name')
    # For some reason, we have to set the password *after* creating the user
    password = factory.PostGenerationMethodCall('set_password', 'password123')

    @factory.post_generation
    def groups(self, create, extracted, **extra):
        targets = extracted if extracted is not None else Group.objects.all()[:3]
        for group in targets:
            self.group_set.add(group)
```

With the current code, a user may do:

```python
UserFactory(password='test')  # Uses 'test' as the password
UserFactory(groups=[some_group])  # Uses the given groups
```

On the other hand, when typing:
```python
UserFactory(
    password=factory.PostGenerationMethodCall('set_unusable_password')
)
```

One would expect that this changes the password generation to call ``set_unusable_password``, instead of trying to use the ``PostGenerationMethodCall`` object as a password (unlikely to work :wink:).

## Issue
This means that post-generation declarations aren't handled as pre-generation are:
- For the latter, passing in any value will override the previous declaration
- With the former, the method will be overridden if the value is itself a post-generation object; if it's anything else, it will be passed to the post-generation object.

This means that:
- It is hard to disable a post-generation hook (unless its code is designed to not run when a specific value is provided)
- The documentation has to be more complicated on that topic; and the barrier to entry steeper.

## Options
I see a few options to address this point:

1. Keep going as presently done
2. Use a specific marker to pass a parameter to the post-generation hook, e.g ``UserFactory(password__='test')``
3. Require that all post-generation methods accept keyword parameters, and pass options as such: ``UserFactory(password__cleartext='test')``

`DjangoModelFactory._get_or_create()` doesn't appear to have any support for the `unique_together` attribute of `Model.Meta`. Perhaps it'd be a good idea to allow `unique_together` tuples in `DjangoModelFactory.Meta.django_get_or_create`?

I'll attach a PR to illustrate a potential approach. Can modify/add tests as necessary.

Thanks!

issue_title: np.nan removed when using the context manager
issue_body:
To reproduce: 
```python
import numpy as np
from numpy import ndarray

from qcodes import ParamSpec, new_data_set, new_experiment
from qcodes.dataset.measurements import Measurement
from qcodes.dataset.data_export import get_data_by_id

from qcodes.dataset.experiment_container import new_experiment

new_experiment("nan_types", "nan")

meas.register_custom_parameter(
    name="x", unit="V"
)

meas.register_custom_parameter(
    name="m", unit="I", setpoints=("x",)
)

with meas.run() as datasaver:
    datasaver.add_result(("x", [0, 1, 2]), ("m", np.array([0.0, np.nan, 1.0])))

dataset = get_data_by_id(datasaver.run_id)
```
If we inspect the dataset we will see that the np.nan has been removed entirely. This is unacceptable, especially in 2D datasets as it will prevent us from plotting the data in the usual manner.

issue_body:
In exploring the `fastai` library, I tried calling `python` to test some code. While in the `fastai/fastai` directory, this caused a core dump:
```
(fastai) prairie@gold:~/fastai/fastai$ python
Fatal Python error: Py_Initialize: can't initialize sys standard streams
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/prairie/fastai/fastai/io.py", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: attempted relative import with no known parent package
Aborted (core dumped)
```
This doesn't happen with`python2.7`

This is a minor edge case which is easy to avoid, but I pass it on any way. (Not even sure if this is anaconda related.)

Thanks for a terrific `fastai` library and an equally impressive MOOC! Much appreciated.

issue_title: WikiText2 dataset incorrect targets
issue_body:
With torchtext v 0.2.1, It seems that the targets returned by the WikiText2 dataset correspond to the next window of `bptt_len` over the dataset. For a language modeling task, shouldn't the targets be simply the next word? 

concretely: `batch.text =  ['<eos>', '=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III']` and `batch.target =  ['=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III', '=']` and it is my understanding that `batch.target = ['=']` would be correct.


full testing code:
```python

from torchtext import data
from torchtext import datasets

TEXT = data.Field()

train_data, val_data, test_data = datasets.WikiText2.splits(TEXT)

TEXT.build_vocab(train_data, min_freq=10)

train_iter, val_iter, test_iter = data.BPTTIterator.splits(
            (train_data, val_data, test_data),
            batch_size=1, bptt_len=5, repeat=False)

batch = next(iter(train_iter))

def recover_text(vector):
    return [TEXT.vocab.itos[int(v)] for v in vector]

recover_text(batch.text.data.squeeze())  # >>> ['<eos>', '=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III']

recover_text(batch.target.data.squeeze()) # >>> ['=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III', '=']
```

Thanks for the awesome package.

-Austin

issue_title: It seems that transforming PIL.Image to tensor by transforms.ToTensor is not working correctly?
issue_body:
I had problems when using transforms.ToTensor() to a float PIL Image.
And then I checked the codes, which are:
`
    # handle PIL Image
    if pic.mode == 'I':
        img = torch.from_numpy(np.array(pic, np.int32, copy=False))
    elif pic.mode == 'I;16':
        img = torch.from_numpy(np.array(pic, np.int16, copy=False))
    else:
        img = torch.ByteTensor(torch.ByteStorage.from_buffer(pic.tobytes()))
    # PIL image mode: 1, L, P, I, F, RGB, YCbCr, RGBA, CMYK
    if pic.mode == 'YCbCr':
        nchannel = 3
    elif pic.mode == 'I;16':
        nchannel = 1
    else:
        nchannel = len(pic.mode)
    img = img.view(pic.size[1], pic.size[0], nchannel)
`
In the float mode, 4 bytes are required for each pixel. Thus the buffer would return a bytes array whose size is HxWxCx4.
The code `img = img.view(pic.size[1], pic.size[0], nchannel)` should be only capable of handling the HxWxC size.

It is right?

issue_title: race condition on access_token_expired()
issue_body:
[access_token_expired](https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/blob/9fb7a06a0618aa88d62b980392f77b5a5f91f98f/social_core/storage.py#L94) has the following logic:

    def access_token_expired(self):
        expiration = self.expiration_timedelta()
        return expiration and expiration.total_seconds() <= 0

Why not: 

    class UserMixin(object):
        ...
        RACE_CONDITION_TIMEOUT = 2
        ...
        def access_token_expired(self):
            expiration = self.expiration_timedelta()
            return expiration and expiration.total_seconds() <= RACE_CONDITION_TIMEOUT

The strategies could override this value (thinking, for example, in creating a settings option for Django)

issue_title: access_token is not refreshed when some extra_data fields are missing
issue_body:
# Versions
```
social-auth-app-django==2.1.0
social-auth-core==1.7.0
```

# The problem
When some fields are missing on `UserSocialAuth.extra_data` json, the function to validate if the _access_token_ is expired `social.access_token_expired()` and the function to get a new _access_token_ `social.get_access_token(load_strategy())` will return `None` as the default behavior 
instead of refreshing the token.

# How to reproduce
1. Setup an extra_data UserSocialAuth object without the fields `expires` or `auth_time`.
2. Call any of the two functions `access_token_expired` or `get_access_token`, it will return None instead of trying to refresh the token.

# Proposal
Default to refresh the access_token if not enough information is found.

I could set up a unit test and a fix if we agree on this but I can't promise a date just yet.

issue_title: Offer ability to force google account chooser.
issue_body:
It's possible to force google to display the account chooser screen when the user is redirected to the `auth` endpoint by including a `prompt` query parameter. [This SO post](http://stackoverflow.com/a/14393492/7322725) goes into a bit more detail. 

My app has users logging in and out with different Google accounts, and the default google auth endpoint seems to remember the last account they'd authed with, and sharply bypasses the account chooser. 

I'm able to get the desired behaviour by adding `params['prompt'] = 'select_account'` to `social_core.backends.oauth.BaseOAuth2.auth_params` :

```py
    def auth_params(self, state=None):
        client_id, client_secret = self.get_key_and_secret()
        params = {
            'client_id': client_id,
            'redirect_uri': self.get_redirect_uri(state)
        }
        if self.STATE_PARAMETER and state:
            params['state'] = state
        if self.RESPONSE_TYPE:
            params['response_type'] = self.RESPONSE_TYPE
        
        params['prompt'] = 'select_account' ## <----  Adding in forced account chooser
        return params
```

I'm not exactly sure the best way to make this configurable, being the many levels of abstraction in this lib. Think you could point me in the right direction and I'll submit a PR @omab ?

issue_title: Mechanism for overriding strategy settings in backend.
issue_body:
There does not appear to be any way to set an alternative default `CLEAN_USERNAME_FUNCTION` or adjust other `DjangoStrategy` settings based on which backend/provider is in use.

The `DjangoStrategy` should have some mechanism for a backend/provider implementation to override these settings. 

I discovered this while building a subclass of `social_core.backends.open_id_connect.OpenIdConnectAuth` for Auth0 which provides a unique user ID, that contains a `|` character, it is trivial to modify the Django models to support this in the username field, and my auth backend class. But due to the only option to configure `social_core.pipeline.user.get_username`'s clean behaviour is `strategy.setting('CLEAN_USERNAME_FUNCTION')`, I am currently required to include yet another line in my `settings.py` that I feel should be possible to configure as part of my auth backend class.

issue_title: Improve error reporting experience
issue_body:
Here's an error message thats not uncommon to see:

```
$ pulumi up
Performing changes:

     Type    Name    Status        Info
 *   global  global  unchanged     1 info message

Diagnostics:
  global: global
    info: We failed to locate the entry point for your program: /Users/luke/dd/pulumidemos/test123
    Here's what we think went wrong:
      * Your program looks like it has a build script associated with it ('tsc').
    Pulumi does not run build scripts before running your program. Please run 'tsc', 'yarn build', or 'npm run build' and try again.

error: an unhandled error occurred: Program exited with non-zero exit code: 1
```

A few things we should do to improve this:

- [ ] Don't include “there was an error advancing the preview”. That layer of feedback isn't useful. 
- [ ] Don't include “program exited with non-zero exit code”. This is simply not something that happened from the users perspective. 
- [ ] Avoid included `global:global` (and possibly also avoid the extra indentation) for messages that are general and not tied to a resource.
- [ ] It’s unclear why we report both a info and an error diagnostic here. We should be able to report only one. 

Also - this specific error message would not be necessary in the first place if we addressed https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/958 and avoided the need for an explicit TypeScript build.

issue_title: [rllib] How does the LSTM work?
issue_body:
I'm curious about the `use_lstm` option and the associated `max_seq_len` and `lstm_cell_size` options when configuring a model in RLlib. Specifically, how does the LSTM work and what values does it remember?

The [documentation](https://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/rllib-models.html?highlight=lstm) says that an LSTM cell further processes the original model's output which adds a [linear layer](https://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/rllib-package-ref.html?highlight=lstm) at the end for final output. The diagram below illustrates my understanding:
```
state -> model -> LSTM -> action
```

Is my understanding of the LSTM correct? How does an LSTM at the output of the original model improve agent behavior? To me, it doesn't seem like the LSTM adds much information about the past states.

Thanks for the help, and sorry about the many questions! I'm fairly new to reinforcement learning.

issue_title: ray.get memory is "slow"
issue_body:
TLDR; There's something about memory allocated by ray.get that makes it hard to get that data into PyTorch/TensorFlow fast enough. Numpy arrays can take 9-12ms to ingest, but numpy arrays coming from Ray take 45ms

Deduping from [discussion](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/ray-dev/OWOrmalTxro) in ray-dev to take advantage of post 1990s word-editing technology.

To get a scale of ideal performance, consider that V100 computes Resnet-50 gradient in 100ms for batch 32, and this gradient is about 100MB in size. Waiting for 45 ms to get Ray parameter values into the framework would drop GPU utilization by 30%

https://github.com/diux-dev/cluster/blob/master/yuxin_numpy/tf_numpy_benchmark.py

```
regular numpy array -> PyTorch GPU memory
python tf_numpy_benchmark.py --benchmark=pytorchgpu_from_numpy --allocator=numpy --num-iters=51
pytorch_from_numpy            :   8.1 GB/sec, min: 12.34, median: 12.37, mean: 12.38
pytorch_from_numpy            :   8.1 GB/sec, min: 12.34, median: 12.39, mean: 12.39


regular numpy array -> TensorFlow GPU memory
python tf_numpy_benchmark.py --benchmark=feed_gpu_tensor --allocator=numpy --num-iters=51
feed_gpu_tensor               :   2.1 GB/sec, min: 47.08, median: 47.43, mean: 47.53
feed_gpu_tensor               :   2.1 GB/sec, min: 47.36, median: 47.94, mean: 47.97

regular numpy array -> TensorFlow GPU memory (using tcmalloc)
sudo apt-get install -y google-perftools
export LD_PRELOAD="/usr/lib/libtcmalloc.so.4" 

python tf_numpy_benchmark.py --benchmark=feed_gpu_tensor --allocator=numpy --num-iters=51
feed_gpu_tensor               :   7.9 GB/sec, min: 12.72, median: 12.82, mean: 12.82
feed_gpu_tensor               :   7.9 GB/sec, min: 12.73, median: 13.07, mean: 13.00

page-locked numpy array -> PyTorch GPU memory
python tf_numpy_benchmark.py --benchmark=pytorchgpu_from_numpy --allocator=tfgpu --num-iters=51
pytorch_from_numpy            :  11.1 GB/sec, min:  9.03, median:  9.03, mean:  9.03
pytorch_from_numpy            :  11.1 GB/sec, min:  9.02, median:  9.03, mean:  9.03

page-locked numpy array -> TensorFlow GPU memory
python tf_numpy_benchmark.py --benchmark=feed_gpu_tensor --allocator=tfgpu --num-iters=51
feed_gpu_tensor               :  10.4 GB/sec, min:  9.61, median:  9.70, mean:  9.71
feed_gpu_tensor               :  10.4 GB/sec, min:  9.58, median:  9.74, mean:  9.75


ray numpy array -> PyTorch GPU memory
python tf_numpy_benchmark.py --benchmark=pytorchgpu_from_numpy --allocator=ray --num-iters=51
pytorchgpu_from_numpy         :   2.2 GB/sec, min: 45.56, median: 45.84, mean: 45.91
pytorchgpu_from_numpy         :   2.2 GB/sec, min: 45.73, median: 45.90, mean: 45.97

ray numpy array -> TensorFlow GPU memory
python tf_numpy_benchmark.py --benchmark=feed_gpu_tensor --allocator=ray --num-iters=51
feed_gpu_tensor               :   1.1 GB/sec, min: 91.34, median: 91.84, mean: 91.76
feed_gpu_tensor               :   1.1 GB/sec, min: 89.31, median: 89.80, mean: 90.01

regular numpy array -> TensorFlow GPU memory (using tcmalloc)
sudo apt-get install -y google-perftools
export LD_PRELOAD="/usr/lib/libtcmalloc.so.4" 
python tf_numpy_benchmark.py --benchmark=feed_gpu_tensor --allocator=ray --num-iters=51
feed_gpu_tensor               :   2.0 GB/sec, min: 51.05, median: 51.40, mean: 51.49

```

issue_title: register:  secret hash issue
issue_body:
I have an app pool with a client secret and either I'm doing something wrong, or i've uncovered an issue with the register() method when requiring a client secret.

The instructions for instantiating the Cognito object for register do not include the username parameter (which makes sense, since you are trying to create one).  So self.username is None.

However, the register method calls:
self._add_secret_hash(params, 'SecretHash')
which if you follow the tree is trying to hash self.username + client id + client secret.
BEFORE it creates the user, so it throws a null reference exception.  

I think that the readme needs to state that if you have a client secret, you need to include the username when you instantiate Cognito (or alternatively, modify the implementation to populate the self.username from the one passed in on the register() method BEFORE trying to add secret hash.

issue_title: Is PublicTicketForm exactly public?
issue_body:
I was implementing a helpdesk for my organization and I had few things to discuss with the developers about this project. I was thinking of contributing to the code with exactly some of the thought process that I feel should be an appropriate choice. 

1) The PublicTicketForm isn't exactly public. A logged in user also uses PublicTicketForm. Is there any important logic that I am missing or is it the choice developers made.

2) An authenticated user creating a ticket, who is not a staff should not be concerned about the Priority and Due date fields in the ticket. I think its the job of the staff members replying to the tickets as a response to provide due dates and priority to a particular ticket.

3) An authenticated user logged in to the platform and creating ticket should not be able to edit the Email field. This might prevent spams from authenticated users and get a better understanding of our authenticated users. (I have actually done this, will submit a pull request shortly).

4) All the email tasks are currently run inside the view. Sending emails via an SMTP server takes time and should rather be made asynchronous (Celery and Redis).

5) Options to move the attachments to a cloud service should be provided.

All the above options seem to be a good fit for the product and I am currently working on some of these for my own organization.

Thanks!

issue_title: improvements to public-facing helpdesk
issue_body:
From #569, we got the following comment from @yugensoft, I moved it to a new ticket for better tracking:

> Basically for public-facing customer help, the following options are needed:
> 
> -     Tickets should only be prioritizable (or due date change) and assignable by admins
> -     The email address of the creator shouldn't be shown (privacy is important)
> -     The yellow view tickets panel has css overlap issues on the text (doesn't bother a dev, but things like that hurt confidence from a customer)
> -     The interface is unclear to a customer. I.e. dashboard only makes sense (assigned to you etc) in a JIRA-like context. They are more likely to click "Tickets", but that shows a search interface (but calls it "Query" which isn't as familiar to a customer). It would be better to have an option whereby (for "customer helpdesk mode"), they only see a "Tickets" tab, and that tab just shows a list of tickets, starting with their own. The search panel could be off to the side, or in a separate view, or a button + modal. At the moment it's taking up all of the page, so a customer has to scroll down to see tickets, something that is jarring to them (they expect just to see tickets when they click tickets).
> -     The base email templates should probably be editable in the database too (otherwise the user is having to fork just to edit that template).
> 
> Hope that info was useful, cheers

issue_title: [v3] aiohttp-json-rpc has version conflict with aiohttp
issue_body:
### Type:

- [ ] Suggestion
- [x] Bug

### Brief description of the problem

Current requirements: yarl==0.18.0, **aiohttp>=2.0.0,<2.3.0**, appdirs, youtube_dl, raven, colorama, aiohttp-json-rpc, pyyaml, Red-Trivia
Installing aiohttp-json-rpc requires `aiohttp>=3,<3.1`, see here: https://github.com/pengutronix/aiohttp-json-rpc/blob/0a36f049e924994353f0de9064b4136f845858ec/setup.py

### Expected behavior

Installing Redv3 works fine and you can run `redbot-setup`.

### Actual behavior

It throws an error and asks for aiohttp>=3,<3.1: https://cdn.discordapp.com/attachments/387398816317440000/413839043277881345/unknown.png

### Steps to reproduce

1. Install Redv3
2. python3 -m pip install aiohttp-json-rpc==0.9.1
3. Run `redbot-setup`

### Fix

Lock aiohttp-json-rpc to an older version.

### Brief description of the problem
There are some nice test fixtures in `tests/conftest.py`, but no good way to import them for use with third party cogs. These tools should made available in the pip deployment for conscientious cog developers.

As an example, I tried copying that file to `site-packages/redbot/testing/conftest.py` and then having `from redbot.testing.conftest import *` in my cog repo's test file. issue_title: Data Management, representation and implementation
issue_body:
We are moving away from a config definition that is used by the DFK to initialize resources to a model in which the user uses a `Config` class that takes initialized resources as parameters. This discussion is here : https://github.com/Parsl/parsl/issues/133. Each executor in this model represents an elastic pool of compute resources. How data assets/methods should map to this model is unclear given only preliminary work has been done on the representation of data management in our current static config based system.
 
Some terminology
1. We have the concept of a `DataManager(DM)` which is an executor private to the DFK. The DM is simply responsible for moving files
2. An `endpoint(EP)` is a block of attributes that define the transfer methods and details like filepaths, ports, auth.
3. An `executor` represents a collection of resources gathered from a computation site. For a single computation site like NERSC you could have several executors that handle specific queues, node requirements etc.

The DM assumes that it can move files between any pair of EPs. Since the DM is generally hidden from the user one possibility is that it can be a default kwarg like this : 
`Config(data_manager=DataManager(threads=4), .... )`

If the EP definition naturally matches to the notion of an executor, the new class structure and our implementation of the executors should be updated to match that. I do not know if this is the case.

<Put picture here>

issue_body:
For reporting purposes we need to capture the following information

 - [ ] Hash of Username, hostname (ip) 
 - [ ] Hash of Parsl script (if possible)
 - [ ] Count of apps launched by DFK
 - [ ] Site IDs used
 - [ ] Total run time

A UDP packet with this info could be sent out from the Parsl script and this could be captured and logged on a server hosted on AWS.
Here are the components we'll need
 - [ ] Log info on the client side and send a UDP packet
 - [ ] Receive and log info to stable storage on our side.
 - [ ] Config option to opt-out of tracking
 - [ ] Documentation to clearly indicate the info collected and how to opt-out

issue_title: Zipkin Exporter rounds timestamps to nearest second. Why?
issue_body:
Hello!

I'm researching using opencensus-python as a means to add tracing to applications I'm helping to develop.

One issue I noticed pretty quickly is that in the "translate_to_zipkin" method in the ZipkinExporter class, the start timestamp and durations variables exported to zipkin are rounded to the nearest second, then converted to microsecond units by multiplying to 1,000,000.  While these variables are converted to microsecond units, they always have 6 trailing zeros regardless of the start and end date times.  Here is the code that's doing this -- noting the start_timestamp_ms and end_timestamp_ms:

>        for span in spans:
>            # Timestamp in zipkin spans is int of microseconds.
>            start_datetime = datetime.datetime.strptime(
>                span.get('startTime'),
>                ISO_DATETIME_REGEX)
>            start_timestamp_ms = calendar.timegm(
>                start_datetime.timetuple()) * 1000 * 1000
>
>            end_datetime = datetime.datetime.strptime(
>                span.get('endTime'),
>                ISO_DATETIME_REGEX)
>            end_timestamp_ms = calendar.timegm(
>                end_datetime.timetuple()) * 1000 * 1000
>
>            duration_ms = end_timestamp_ms - start_timestamp_ms

Python datetime objects can have microsecond precision.  
Zipkin metrics can also handle microsecond precision.  

To utilize the microsecond precision I wanted, I had to derive my own version of the ZipkinExporter class to override this method. So I calculated the duration as such:

>        for span in spans:
>            # Timestamp in zipkin spans is int of microseconds.
>           start_datetime = datetime.datetime.strptime(
>                span.get('startTime'),
>                ISO_DATETIME_REGEX)
>
>            end_datetime = datetime.datetime.strptime(
>                span.get('endTime'),
>                ISO_DATETIME_REGEX)
>
>            duration_ms = int((end_datetime - start_datetime).total_seconds() * 1000 * 1000)

The conversion I changed still involves multiplying by 1,000,000, but it's multiplied to a seconds float value instead of a rounded int as above.

As for setting the start timestamp, in the class I created, I have an epoch datetime constant like this. Since the current code assumes UTC date times, I'm assuming the same:

> epoch = datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp(0)

..and for converting the start_datetime variable to epoch microseconds, I do this:

> 'timestamp': int((start_datetime - self.epoch).total_seconds() * 1000 * 1000)

(The above conversion of a timedelta to epoch microseconds can be easily wrapped into a small static method.)

The precision of both the start timestamp and the duration in the class I created now utilizes true microsecond precision with these changes.

Is there a good reason the current code for ZipkinExporter needs to round to whole seconds before exporting?

- Brian

issue_title: textX + PyEcore
issue_body:
**Note:** _In this issue, are reported discussions and experiments around the association of [textX](https://github.com/igordejanovic/textX) and PyEcore. Progress can be found [here](https://github.com/aranega/textX) and it currently requires the PyEcore version from the `develop` branch._

TextX is a meta-language, inspired by [Xtext](http://www.eclipse.org/Xtext/) which allows you to easily generate a parser and an metamodel/model-based AST for a textual language. The metamodel based AST created by textX is not yet compatible with the Ecore API. This POC tries to bring PyEcore, so Ecore, to textX in order to be able to handle an Ecore compatible model and metamodel. 

This addition could open a way of interoperability between Java-EMF and Python-EMF (PyEcore) through the xmi/ecore file format. Moreover, using `pyecoregen`, it could enable a full Python T2M (Text to Model), and M2T (Model to Text) experience. 

The goal of this POC is to see if PyEcore can be integrated inside textX following three scenarios:

1. from a textX grammar, an Ecore metamodel is automatically generated, and the generated parser + metamodel can be directly used to build the AST.
2. using existing dynamic Ecore metamodel (built in memory) and a textX grammar, textX uses the defined dynamic Ecore metamodel to build the AST.
3. using a first compilation phase: a textX grammar is used for generating the Python Ecore metamodel code (through the textX cli). Then the generated Python code is used by textX to build the AST. 

This latter scenario allows the user to easily add behavior to the metaclasses instances.

The scenario 2 had been already validated. This implied minor modifications of how textX create instances and init them (no need to init instances anymore as PyEcore does it), and a biggest modifications on how the cross-references are resolved. 

The scenario 1 and 3 relies on the same principle: the Ecore metamodel must be generated on-the-fly from the textX grammar. Consequently, the isses and challenges are not the same as in scenario 2.

Here what's have been validated so far and the future features to add:

- [X] Replace textX metaclasses creation by `EClass` instances
- [X] Add `EAttribute/EReference` support for `EClass`
- [X] Reference `eOpposite` support for static and dynamic Ecore metamodel (not automatically generated at the moment)
- [X] Deal with `EClass` inheritance
- [X] Deal with abstract classes
- [X] EPackage creation for a grammar/metamodel
- [X] Detection of rules that define `EDataType`
- [X] Creation of `EDataType` 
- [X] Detection of rules that define `EEnum` (currently not bullet-proof)
- [X] Creation/Filling of `EEnum`
- [X] Basic data types support (should be modified)
- [ ] Management of split grammar (defined in many files)
- [ ] Management of multiple namespace/`EPackage`
- [ ] Add a cli option to generate the PyEcore metamodel using `pyecoregen`.

As a complement of this list, preferably, the PyEcore integration is optional and can be activated using a parameter in textX. Also, another idea would be to add a dedicated keyword to introduce `eOpposite` from grammar.

The generation of the metamodel Python code using the cli also implies the question of re-generation and perservation of manually inserted modifications. This issue is more general and could be addressed in `pyecoregen` or [pymultigen](https://github.com/moltob/pymultigen).

issue_title: 'File is missing' error accessing a file in a Directory, Toil expects a file:// URI which failed to be added
issue_body:
We are experiencing failures using Toil to run our CWL workflow, and suspect a bug in Toil's handling of Directory inputs. The symptom is an error message, "File is missing: /the/path/to/some/file" (note that this is a file path, without an IRI schema prefix).

On code inspection of `uploadFile()` in `src/toil/cwl/cwltoil.py`, it is clear the logic expects an IRI with `file://` schema prefix within the contents of `uf["location"]`. trimming the first 7 characters in the check `if not os.path.isfile(uf["location"][7:]):`. That prefix should have been added when resolving the IRI relative to the CWL on the filesystem, and clearly the error message shows it was not. Since the first 7 characters of the file path get stripped regardless of the schema prefix, the path fails to represent a valid file in the filesystem.

The code paths for resolving IRIs for File vs Directory are different, and I suspect a bug in the latter. In particular, File IRIs have logic to resolve schema-relative locations, via a call to `schema_salad.ref_resolver.file_uri()`; suspiciously, such call isn't present in the code path for resolving Directory objects.

To reproduce this failure, you may try to run the NCBI PGAP workflow. Please note that this pipeline is still a work in progress, and has not yet been formally announced or released; the URL is subject to move:
https://github.com/ncbi-gpipe/pgap

issue_title: csvw.dsv roundtrip failiure with escapechar and QUOTE_NONE
issue_body:
```python
>>> import csv
>>> from csvw.dsv import UnicodeWriter, UnicodeReader
>>> kwargs = {'quoting': csv.QUOTE_NONE, 'escapechar': '\\'}
>>> row = ['\\spam']
>>> with UnicodeWriter(**kwargs) as writer:
	writer.writerow(row)
>>> writer.f.seek(0)
0
>>> with UnicodeReader(writer.f, **kwargs) as reader:
	result = next(reader)
>>> assert result == row
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
AssertionError
>>> result
['\\\\spam']
```

https://bugs.python.org/issue12178 only occurs with quoting dialects, so our workaround for it `_escapedoubled()` needs to be restricted to those dialects:
https://github.com/cldf/csvw/blob/ecc8e74b53c7478b83d32d1c0fc19e2df4c7288f/csvw/dsv.py#L99-L109

issue_title: Incorrect handling of backslashes in Git configuration
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce the issue:

    import git
    config = git.Repo().config_writer()
    config.add_section('test')
    config.set_value('test', 'test', r'some\data')

Now if you try to read this value using a regular (non Python) `git config`, Git complains that the configuration file is invalid:

    fatal: bad config file line 11 in .git/config

Indeed, if you open `.git/config` you can see that the value is written as:

    [test]
      test = some\data

While the [`git-config` configuration]() states that:

> String values may be entirely or partially enclosed in double quotes. You need to enclose variable values in double quotes if you want to preserve leading or trailing whitespace, or if the variable value contains comment characters (i.e. it contains # or ;). Double quote " and backslash \ characters in variable values must be escaped: use \" for " and \\ for \.

That is, the backslashes are not escaped in the configuration file.

This also cause issues regarding the reading, where values are not un-escaped.
